summary: Gruber reviews the Apple Studio Display, the long-awaited sub-$2000 standalone monitor that addresses years of neglect in Apple's display lineup. The screen, audio, integration, and industrial design are excellent — approaching perfection for a MacBook docking station experience. However, the built-in camera is crushingly disappointing, producing grainy, washed-out images far worse than the same camera hardware in a $600 iPad Air. Center Stage is glitchy and ill-suited for a fixed desktop display. Apple acknowledged the issues and promised software fixes, though Gruber is skeptical they'll be sufficient. Despite the camera failure, he's buying one anyway because everything else meets or exceeds expectations.
key_points: The Studio Display corrects the final major gap in Apple's Mac lineup after years of fixing the Mac Pro, butterfly keyboards, MacBook Air, and the Intel-to-Apple-silicon transition
Screen quality, speaker audio, microphone array, and single-cable MacBook integration are all excellent, with the no-power-brick design being a smart tradeoff for slightly more thickness

The camera produces embarrassingly poor image quality compared to the same hardware in iPads and even the $430 iPhone SE's front-facing camera, suggesting a software/ISP processing failure rather than hardware limitation
Center Stage is a poor fit for a fixed desktop display and its implementation is buggy, with multi-second delays in reframing
Apple acknowledged camera quality issues and promised software fixes, with sources suggesting it may be a last-minute bug rather than a fundamental hardware problem
money_quotes: The final wrong Apple needed to make right was a standalone display.
That's like saying a vehicle company that had only been making trucks returned to the car market with a $150,000 race car.

One of these is from a $600 iPad. The other from a $1600 display. Both are using the same camera hardware. It doesn't make sense that they look so different, and makes less sense that the one that looks like ass is from the $1600 product.
This image quality is embarrassing from a company that considers itself the leading camera company in the world.
The Center Stage feature on the Studio Display should be called Off-Center Stage.

On the Studio Display, it's as though that cameraperson is distracted, paying attention to something else, and then they realize, oops, you — the subject they're supposed to be following — are framed poorly, and they sheepishly pan the camera to reframe you.
I expected to be impressed by the Studio Display camera. Instead, I'm baffled. I don't understand how this shipped.

I spent most of the space in this review discussing and illustrating my disappointment in the camera, but I don't know how to say more to express my joy with, for example, the audio quality of the speakers and the visual quality of the screen.
key_insight: The Studio Display is nearly everything Mac users waited years for, but its inexplicably poor camera — using identical hardware that performs well in cheaper Apple products — represents a baffling quality control failure from a company that prides itself on camera excellence.
